---
title: "Final_QMBA_Project"
output:
  pdf_document: default
  html_document: default
  word_document: default
date: "2026-04-13"
---

```{r setup}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)
library(readxl)
library(ggplot2)
library(corrplot)
library(car)
customer_data <- read_excel("./outputs/26-04-14-16/customer_data_cleaned.xlsx")

# Convert columns that should be numeric but may be stored as text
numeric_cols <- c(
  "Region", "Age", "Education Years", "Employment Years", "Household Size",
  "Household Income", "Number Pets", "umber Pets", "Home Owner", "Car Value",
  "Commute Distance", "Credit Card Tenure", "TV Watching Hours",
  "Coupon Redemption", "Brand Tenure Months", "Monthly Spend ProductA",
  "Cumulative Spend ProductA", "Monthly Spend ProductB", "Cumulative Spend ProductB",
  "Monthly Spend ProductC", "Cumulative Spend ProductC", "Total Avg Monthly Spend",
  "High Value Customer"
)

numeric_cols <- intersect(numeric_cols, names(customer_data))

safe_numeric <- function(x) {
  as.numeric(gsub("[^0-9.-]", "", as.character(x)))
}

customer_data[numeric_cols] <- lapply(customer_data[numeric_cols], safe_numeric)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))
```

```{r overall_data_check}
head(customer_data)
str(customer_data)
summary(customer_data)
sum(is.na(customer_data))
```

```{r where_are_the_3_na}
na_rows_productA <- which(is.na(customer_data$`Cumulative Spend ProductA`))
na_rows_productA
customer_data[na_rows_productA, c("CustomerID", "Cumulative Spend ProductA")]
```

```{r region_frequency}
summary(customer_data$Region)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = factor(Region, levels = 1:5))) +
  geom_bar(fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  geom_text(stat = "count", aes(label = after_stat(count)), vjust = -0.3, size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Region Frequency Count", x = "Region", y = "Count")
```

```{r age_hist}
summary(customer_data$Age)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = Age)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, fill = "lightblue", color = "black", boundary = 0) +
  labs(title = "Age", x = "Age", y = "Count")
```

```{r education_years_hist}
summary(customer_data$`Education Years`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = `Education Years`)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  geom_text(stat = "count", aes(label = after_stat(count)), vjust = -0.3, size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Education Years", x = "Years", y = "Count")
```

```{r employment_years_hist}
summary(customer_data$`Employment Years`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = `Employment Years`)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, fill = "lightblue", color = "black", boundary = 0) +
  labs(title = "Employment Years", x = "Years", y = "Count")
```

```{r household_size_hist}
summary(customer_data$`Household Size`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = factor(`Household Size`))) +
  geom_bar(fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  geom_text(stat = "count", aes(label = after_stat(count)), vjust = -0.3, size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Household Size", x = "Size", y = "Count")

```


```{r household_income_hist}
summary(customer_data$`Household Income`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = `Household Income`)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::label_comma()) +
  labs(title = "Household Income", x = "Income", y = "Count")
```

```{r number_pets_hist}
pet_col <- if ("Number Pets" %in% names(customer_data)) "Number Pets" else "umber Pets"
summary(customer_data[[pet_col]])
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = .data[[pet_col]])) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  labs(title = "Number Pets", x = "Pets", y = "Count")
```

```{r home_owner_boxplot}
summary(customer_data$`Home Owner`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = factor(`Home Owner`))) +
  geom_bar(fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  geom_text(stat = "count", aes(label = after_stat(count)), vjust = -0.3, size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Home Owner (0/1)", x = "Home Owner", y = "Count")
```

```{r car_value_hist}
summary(customer_data$`Car Value`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = `Car Value`)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  labs(title = "Car Value", x = "Car Value", y = "Count")
```

```{r commute_distance_hist}
customer_data$`Commute Distance`[
  !grepl("^[0-9.]+$", customer_data$`Commute Distance`)
] <- NA

customer_data$`Commute Distance` <- as.numeric(customer_data$`Commute Distance`)
summary(customer_data$`Commute Distance`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x =`Commute Distance`)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, fill = "lightblue", color = "black", boundary = 0) +
  labs(title = "Commute Distance", x = "Distance", y = "Count")

```

```{r credit_card_tenure_hist}
summary(customer_data$`Credit Card Tenure`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = `Credit Card Tenure`)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, fill = "lightblue", color = "black", boundary = 0) +
  labs(title = "Credit Card Tenure", x = "Tenure", y = "Count")
```

```{r tv_hours_hist}
summary(customer_data$`TV Watching Hours`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = `TV Watching Hours`)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, fill = "lightblue", color = "black", boundary = 0) +
  labs(title = "TV Watching Hours", x = "Hours", y = "Count")
```

```{r coupon_redemption_boxplot}
summary(customer_data$`Coupon Redemption`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = factor(`Coupon Redemption`))) +
  geom_bar(fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  geom_text(stat = "count", aes(label = after_stat(count)), vjust = -0.3, size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Coupon Redemption (0/1)", x = "Coupon Redemption", y = "Count")
```

```{r brand_tenure_hist}
summary(customer_data$`Brand Tenure Months`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = `Brand Tenure Months`)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  labs(title = "Brand Tenure Months", x = "Months", y = "Count")
```

```{r monthly_a_hist}
summary(customer_data$`Monthly Spend ProductA`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = `Monthly Spend ProductA`)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  labs(title = "Monthly Spend ProductA", x = "Spend", y = "Count")
```

```{r cumulative_a_hist}
summary(customer_data$`Cumulative Spend ProductA`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = `Cumulative Spend ProductA`)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  labs(title = "Cumulative Spend ProductA", x = "Spend", y = "Count")
```

```{r monthly_b_hist}
summary(customer_data$`Monthly Spend ProductB`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = `Monthly Spend ProductB`)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  labs(title = "Monthly Spend ProductB", x = "Spend", y = "Count")
```

```{r cumulative_b_hist}
summary(customer_data$`Cumulative Spend ProductB`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = `Cumulative Spend ProductB`)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  labs(title = "Cumulative Spend ProductB", x = "Spend", y = "Count")
```

```{r monthly_c_hist}
summary(customer_data$`Monthly Spend ProductC`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = `Monthly Spend ProductC`)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  labs(title = "Monthly Spend ProductC", x = "Spend", y = "Count")
```

```{r cumulative_c_hist}
summary(customer_data$`Cumulative Spend ProductC`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = `Cumulative Spend ProductC`)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  labs(title = "Cumulative Spend ProductC", x = "Spend", y = "Count")
```

```{r total_avg_monthly_hist}
summary(customer_data$`Total Avg Monthly Spend`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = `Total Avg Monthly Spend`)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  labs(title = "Total Avg Monthly Spend", x = "Spend", y = "Count")
```

```{r high_value_customer_boxplot}
summary(customer_data$`High Value Customer`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = factor(`High Value Customer`))) +
  geom_bar(fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  geom_text(stat = "count", aes(label = after_stat(count)), vjust = -0.3, size = 3) +
  labs(title = "High Value Customer (0/1)", x = "High Value Customer", y = "Count")
```

```{r gender_summary}
summary(customer_data$Gender)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = Gender)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  geom_text(stat = "count", aes(label = after_stat(count)), vjust = -0.3, size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Gender Frequency Count", x = "Gender", y = "Count")
```

```{r job_category_summary}
summary(customer_data$`Job Category`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = `Job Category`)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  geom_text(stat = "count", aes(label = after_stat(count)), vjust = -0.3, size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Job Category Frequency Count", x = "Job Category", y = "Count")
```

```{r marital_status_summary}
summary(customer_data$`Marital Status`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = `Marital Status`)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  geom_text(stat = "count", aes(label = after_stat(count)), vjust = -0.3, size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Marital Status Frequency Count", x = "Marital Status", y = "Count")
```

```{r car_ownership_summary}
summary(customer_data$`Car Ownership`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = `Car Ownership`)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  geom_text(stat = "count", aes(label = after_stat(count)), vjust = -0.3, size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Car Ownership Frequency Count", x = "Car Ownership", y = "Count")
```

```{r car_brand_summary}
summary(customer_data$`Car Brand`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = `Car Brand`)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  geom_text(stat = "count", aes(label = after_stat(count)), vjust = -0.3, size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Car Brand Frequency Count", x = "Car Brand", y = "Count")
```

```{r political_party_summary}
summary(customer_data$`Political Party`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = `Political Party`)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  geom_text(stat = "count", aes(label = after_stat(count)), vjust = -0.3, size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Political Party Frequency Count", x = "Political Party", y = "Count")
```

```{r votes_summary}
summary(customer_data$Votes)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = Votes)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  geom_text(stat = "count", aes(label = after_stat(count)), vjust = -0.3, size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Votes Frequency Count", x = "Votes", y = "Count")
```

```{r active_lifestyle_summary}
summary(customer_data$`Active Lifestyle`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = `Active Lifestyle`)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  geom_text(stat = "count", aes(label = after_stat(count)), vjust = -0.3, size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Active Lifestyle Frequency Count", x = "Active Lifestyle", y = "Count")
```

```{r streaming_summary}
summary(customer_data$`Streaming Svcs`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = `Streaming Svcs`)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  geom_text(stat = "count", aes(label = after_stat(count)), vjust = -0.3, size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Streaming Services Frequency Count", x = "Streaming Services", y = "Count")
```

```{r wireless_summary}
summary(customer_data$`Wireless Internet`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = `Wireless Internet`)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  geom_text(stat = "count", aes(label = after_stat(count)), vjust = -0.3, size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Wireless Internet Frequency Count", x = "Wireless Internet", y = "Count")
```

```{r smartphone_summary}
summary(customer_data$`Smart Phone`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = `Smart Phone`)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  geom_text(stat = "count", aes(label = after_stat(count)), vjust = -0.3, size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Smart Phone Frequency Count", x = "Smart Phone", y = "Count")
```

```{r twitter_summary}
summary(customer_data$`Twitter Acct`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = `Twitter Acct`)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  geom_text(stat = "count", aes(label = after_stat(count)), vjust = -0.3, size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Twitter Account Frequency Count", x = "Twitter Account", y = "Count")
```

```{r linkedin_summary}
summary(customer_data$`LinkedIn Acct`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = `LinkedIn Acct`)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  geom_text(stat = "count", aes(label = after_stat(count)), vjust = -0.3, size = 3) +
  labs(title = "LinkedIn Account Frequency Count", x = "LinkedIn Account", y = "Count")
```

```{r facebook_summary}
summary(customer_data$`Facebook Acct`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = `Facebook Acct`)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  geom_text(stat = "count", aes(label = after_stat(count)), vjust = -0.3, size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Facebook Account Frequency Count", x = "Facebook Account", y = "Count")
```

```{r news_subscriber_summary}
summary(customer_data$`News Subscriber`)
ggplot(customer_data, aes(x = `News Subscriber`)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "lightblue", color = "black") +
  geom_text(stat = "count", aes(label = after_stat(count)), vjust = -0.3, size = 3) +
  labs(title = "News Subscriber Frequency Count", x = "News Subscriber", y = "Count")
```

```{r simple_correlation_numeric}
num_data <- customer_data[sapply(customer_data, is.numeric)]
corr_mat <- round(cor(num_data, use = "pairwise.complete.obs"), 3)
corr_mat
```

```{r simple_correlation_circles_heatmap, fig.width=10, fig.height=8}
# Larger figure and adjusted text sizes to reduce crowding
corrplot(
  corr_mat,
  method = "circle",
  type = "lower",
  order = "hclust",
  tl.col = "black",
  tl.srt = 45,
  tl.cex = 0.8,       # label text size
  cl.cex = 0.9,       # color legend text size
  number.cex = 0.6,   # coefficient text size (if shown)
  addCoef.col = NULL, # hide numeric coefficients to avoid overlap
  col = colorRampPalette(c("#2166ac", "white", "#b2182b"))(200),
  mar = c(0, 0, 2, 0), # a bit more top margin for the title
  title = "Simple Correlation Plot"
)
```

```{r top_correlations_with_total_avg_monthly_spend}
# Top correlations with target variable for linear regression selection
target_var <- "Total Avg Monthly Spend"

target_corr <- cor(num_data, use = "pairwise.complete.obs")[, target_var]
target_corr <- target_corr[names(target_corr) != target_var]

top_corr <- data.frame(
  variable = names(target_corr),
  correlation = as.numeric(target_corr),
  abs_correlation = abs(as.numeric(target_corr))
)

top_corr <- top_corr[order(-top_corr$abs_correlation), ]
head(top_corr, 10)
```

```{r logistic_regression_auto, echo=TRUE}
# Automatic predictor filtering and logistic regression

# In R formulas, spaces cause problems, so this function wraps those names in backticks:
safe_name <- function(x) {
  if (grepl("^[A-Za-z0-9_.]+$", x)) x else paste0("`", x, "`")
}

#Here are the columns I want (Independent Vars)
model_vars <- c(
  "High Value Customer",
  "Total Avg Monthly Spend",
  "Household Income",
  "Age",
  "Household Size",
  "Home Owner",
  "Credit Card Tenure",
  "Brand Tenure Months",
  "Coupon Redemption",
  "Streaming Svcs",
  "Wireless Internet",
  "Smart Phone"
)
# Only keep the ones that actually exist in the dataset (Optional Robust Check)
model_vars <- intersect(model_vars, names(customer_data))
# Copy rows into new smaller dataframe
mod_data <- customer_data[ , model_vars]

# Ensure logistic model outcome is binary factor 0/1
mod_data$`High Value Customer` <- as.factor(ifelse(as.numeric(mod_data$`High Value Customer`)>0, 1, 0))

# 1) Drop columns with all NA or single unique value
# This checks whether a variable is basically useless because it has only one value.
# 
# Example:
# 
# if every person had Home Owner = 1
# then that variable tells us nothing
# 
# So the code removes columns that are:
# 
# all missing, or
# only one unique value
nzv <- sapply(mod_data, function(x) length(unique(na.omit(x))) <= 1)
if (any(nzv)) cat("Dropping no-variation vars:", paste(names(nzv)[nzv], collapse=", "), "\n")
mod_data <- mod_data[ , !nzv, drop=FALSE]

# 2) Near-zero variance for factors / zero sd for numerics
# This is similar, but a little smarter.
# 
# It asks:
# 
# “Is this variable almost always the same value?”
# 
# For numeric variables:
# 
# if standard deviation is 0, remove it
# 
# For categorical variables:
# 
# if one category makes up more than 99.5% of the data, remove it
# Because if a variable is almost constant, it usually doesn’t help the model and can create instability.
nearzero <- sapply(names(mod_data), function(nm){
  if (nm=="High Value Customer") return(FALSE)
  x <- mod_data[[nm]]
  xnm <- na.omit(x)
  if (length(xnm)==0) return(TRUE)
  if (is.numeric(xnm)) return(sd(xnm, na.rm=TRUE)==0)
  tbl <- table(xnm)
  max(tbl)/sum(tbl) > 0.995
})
if (any(nearzero)) { cat("Dropping near-zero vars:", paste(names(nearzero)[nearzero], collapse=", "), "\n"); mod_data <- mod_data[ , !nearzero, drop=FALSE] }

# 3) Perfect separation checks
# This is one of the most important parts.
# 
# What is perfect separation?
# 
# It means one predictor can almost perfectly split the outcome.
# 
# Example:
# 
# everyone with X > 100 is a high-value customer
# everyone with X <= 100 is not
# 
# That sounds great, but in logistic regression it actually causes problems:
# 
# coefficients blow up
# model becomes unstable
# standard errors get weird
# 
# So the code searches for variables that separate 0s and 1s too perfectly, then drops them.
# 
# That’s why your output said:
# 
# Dropping perfect/separating vars: Total Avg Monthly Spend
# 
# That means this variable was almost too good at identifying the outcome.
sep_vars <- character()
for (nm in setdiff(names(mod_data), "High Value Customer")){
  x <- mod_data[[nm]]
  if (is.numeric(x)){
    a0 <- x[mod_data$`High Value Customer`==0]
    a1 <- x[mod_data$`High Value Customer`==1]
    if (length(na.omit(a0))>0 && length(na.omit(a1))>0){
      if (max(a0, na.rm=TRUE) < min(a1, na.rm=TRUE) || max(a1, na.rm=TRUE) < min(a0, na.rm=TRUE)){
        sep_vars <- c(sep_vars, nm)
      }
    }
  } else {
    tbl <- table(x, mod_data$`High Value Customer`)
    if (nrow(tbl)>0 && any(apply(tbl,1,function(r) r[1]==0 || r[2]==0))) sep_vars <- c(sep_vars, nm)
  }
}
if (length(sep_vars)>0){ cat("Dropping perfect/separating vars:", paste(unique(sep_vars), collapse=", "), "\n"); mod_data <- mod_data[ , !(names(mod_data) %in% sep_vars), drop=FALSE] }

# 4) High pairwise correlation among numerics (>0.9): drop the lower-variance member
# This checks for multicollinearity.
# 
# That means:
# 
# two predictors are telling almost the same story
# 
# Example:
# 
# Credit Card Tenure and Brand Tenure Months might move together a lot
# 
# If two variables correlate above 0.9, the code drops one of them.
# Because highly correlated predictors can:
# 
# make coefficients unstable
# inflate standard errors
# make interpretation messy
# 
# The code keeps the one with higher variance and drops the other.
# 
# That’s why your output said:
# 
# Dropping highly correlated numeric vars: Credit Card Tenure
num_idx <- sapply(mod_data, is.numeric)
num_idx["High Value Customer"] <- FALSE
num_names <- names(mod_data)[num_idx]
if (length(num_names)>1){
  cor_mat <- cor(mod_data[ , num_names], use="pairwise.complete.obs")
  high_pairs <- which(abs(cor_mat) > 0.9 & abs(cor_mat) < 1, arr.ind=TRUE)
  drop_cols <- character()
  if (nrow(high_pairs)>0){
    for (i in seq_len(nrow(high_pairs))){
      a <- rownames(cor_mat)[high_pairs[i,1]]
      b <- colnames(cor_mat)[high_pairs[i,2]]
      var_a <- var(mod_data[[a]], na.rm=TRUE); var_b <- var(mod_data[[b]], na.rm=TRUE)
      drop_cols <- c(drop_cols, ifelse(var_a < var_b, a, b))
    }
    drop_cols <- unique(drop_cols)
    cat("Dropping highly correlated numeric vars:", paste(drop_cols, collapse=", "), "\n")
    mod_data <- mod_data[ , !(names(mod_data) %in% drop_cols), drop=FALSE]
  }
}

# 5) Iterative VIF-based removal if possible (silent if car not installed)
# This part tries to calculate VIF = Variance Inflation Factor.
# 
# VIF tells you whether a variable is too predictable from the other predictors.
# 
# Rule of thumb:
# 
# VIF > 10 = bad multicollinearity
# 
# If that happens,drops those variables too
if (ncol(mod_data) > 2){
  pred_names <- setdiff(names(mod_data), "High Value Customer")
  fterms <- paste(vapply(pred_names, safe_name, character(1)), collapse = " + ")
  f <- as.formula(paste(safe_name("High Value Customer"), "~", fterms))
  mm <- model.matrix(f, data = mod_data)
  mm <- mm[, colnames(mm)!="(Intercept)", drop=FALSE]
  vif_vals <- sapply(seq_len(ncol(mm)), function(i){
    r <- summary(lm(mm[,i] ~ mm[,-i]))$r.squared
    if (is.na(r)) NA else 1/(1-r)
  })
  names(vif_vals) <- colnames(mm)
  high_vif <- names(vif_vals)[which(vif_vals>10)]
  if (length(high_vif)>0){
    orig_drop <- unique(sub("^(.*?)[:].*$", "\\1", high_vif))
    cat("Dropping high-VIF variables (>",10,"):", paste(orig_drop, collapse=", "), "\n")
    mod_data <- mod_data[ , !(names(mod_data) %in% orig_drop), drop=FALSE]
  }
}

# Final model fit
terms <- setdiff(names(mod_data), "High Value Customer")
if (length(terms)==0) stop('No predictors remain after automatic filtering.')
terms_safe <- vapply(terms, safe_name, character(1))
formula_terms <- paste(terms_safe, collapse = " + ")
logit_formula <- as.formula(paste(safe_name("High Value Customer"), "~", formula_terms))
logit_model <- glm(logit_formula, data = mod_data, family = binomial(), na.action = na.omit)

summary(logit_model)
# In logistic regression, coefficients are in log-odds, which are hard to interpret.
# 
# So this converts them to odds ratios, which are easier.
# 
# Example:
# 
# coefficient = 1.077
# odds ratio = exp(1.077) ≈ 2.94
# Smart Phone → Odds Ratio ≈ 2.94
# 
# People with smartphones are about 2.94 times more likely to be high-value customers
# Odds Ratio = 2.94  
# CI = [2.10, 4.10]
# 
# We are 95% (confint R func convention) confident the true odds ratio is between 2.10 and 4.10
odds <- exp(coef(logit_model))
ci <- tryCatch(exp(confint(logit_model)), error = function(e) matrix(NA, nrow=length(odds), ncol=2))
odds_table <- data.frame(Estimate = coef(logit_model), OddsRatio = odds, CI_low = ci[,1], CI_high = ci[,2])
print(odds_table)
null_dev <- logit_model$null.deviance
resid_dev <- logit_model$deviance
pseudo_r2 <- 1 - resid_dev/null_dev
cat("Pseudo R-squared:", round(pseudo_r2, 4), "\n")
cat("Observations used:", length(logit_model$fitted.values), "\n")

```
